era_context: The early 8th century CE continues with the Umayyad Caliphate experiencing internal fragmentation, the Tang Dynasty at its zenith, the Byzantine Iconoclastic Controversy ongoing, and the Frankish kingdom consolidating power. This period is marked by religious conflict, military competition, and significant political transitions across Eurasia and North Africa.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe and Scandinavia (sparse written records)
Southeast Asia and India (limited contemporary documentation)

events.title: Umayyad Caliphate Internal Conflict and Factional Warfare
events.region: Umayyad Caliphate (Syria, Iraq, Levant, North Africa)
events.description: The Umayyad Caliphate under al-Walid II continued to experience severe internal conflict in 736. Competing military factions (jund) engaged in open warfare over power and resources. The central authority of the caliph was increasingly challenged by regional governors and military commanders, particularly in Syria and Iraq. This period of instability was a precursor to the eventual collapse of the Umayyad dynasty.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: The broad pattern of Umayyad internal conflict is well-attested, but precise dating of specific engagements to 736 is uncertain.

events.title: Berber Kharijite Uprising Begins in North Africa
events.description: The Kharijite-led Berber uprising in North Africa began or escalated in 736, with Berber forces rising against Umayyad rule. The revolt was driven by Berber grievances over taxation, military conscription, and Arab dominance, combined with Kharijite Islamic theology that emphasized egalitarianism and rejected the authority of the Umayyad caliphate. The uprising would eventually gain significant territorial control across the Maghreb.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings
Ibn Abd al-Hakam, History of Egypt

events.certainty_note: The Berber Revolt is well-attested in Islamic sources, but the precise dating of its beginning is uncertain; sources vary on whether it began in 736 or the late 730s.

events.title: Tang Dynasty Military Campaigns in Central Asia
events.region: Tang China, Central Asia (Tarim Basin, Sogdiana)
events.description: The Tang Dynasty under Emperor Xuanzong continued military campaigns in Central Asia in 736 to maintain control over the Silk Road trade routes. Tang forces engaged with various Central Asian powers to secure Chinese dominance over trade and cultural influence in the region. This period saw the height of Tang military and commercial power in Central Asia.
key_figures: Tang military commanders in Central Asia
events.certainty_note: Tang military activity in Central Asia during this period is well-documented, but specific events in 736 are not clearly distinguished in sources.

events.title: Byzantine Iconoclastic Controversy and Religious Debate
events.description: The Iconoclastic Controversy continued to dominate Byzantine religious and political life in 736. Emperor Leo III maintained his policy of opposing the veneration of religious images, which had been in effect since the early 720s. The controversy divided the Byzantine church and society, with monks and some clergy defending icons while the imperial court and some theologians opposed them.

sources.name: John of Damascus, Apologia Against Those Who Attack the Divine Images

events.certainty_note: The Iconoclastic Controversy is well-attested in Byzantine sources; John of Damascus's writings provide contemporary theological perspective.

events.title: Frankish Kingdom Consolidation under Charles Martel
events.description: In 736, Charles Martel, the Frankish Mayor of the Palace, continued to consolidate power and strengthen the Frankish kingdom. Charles Martel was reorganizing the military structure and administrative systems of the Frankish realm, laying the groundwork for the Carolingian dynasty. Relations with the papacy were becoming increasingly important as the Franks positioned themselves as defenders of the Church against external threats.

sources.name: Liber Historiae Francorum (Book of the History of the Franks)

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of Frankish power is documented in later chronicles; specific events in 736 are not always clearly distinguished.

disconfirming_evidence: The Berber Revolt is sometimes presented as a unified movement with clear leadership and ideology, but modern scholarship emphasizes its fragmented nature and the varying theological and political motivations of different Berber groups. The dating of the Berber Revolt's beginning is uncertain; some sources place it in 736, others in the late 730s, and the distinction between the beginning of the revolt and its escalation is unclear. The Iconoclastic Controversy is often portrayed as a simple binary conflict, but recent scholarship shows the theological positions were more nuanced. The Tang Dynasty's military campaigns in Central Asia are sometimes presented as unambiguous 'expansion,' but recent scholarship emphasizes the contested nature of these territories and the agency of local Central Asian powers.
historiographic_note: The year 736 is moderately well-documented for the Islamic world and Tang China, with sources compiled 150–300 years after events. Byzantine sources are somewhat better preserved, with some contemporary theological writings. Frankish sources are fragmentary and often written 100–400 years after events. The sources are predominantly elite-focused and reflect the perspectives of court, church, or military leadership. Sub-Saharan Africa, Scandinavia, and the Americas are absent from contemporary documentation. There is a significant bias toward documenting political and military events over social, economic, or cultural developments. The dating of the Berber Revolt's beginning is particularly uncertain, with different sources placing it at different points in the 730s.

graph_edges.note: Umayyad factional conflict continued and intensified
Berber Revolt continued to spread across North Africa
Tang military campaigns in Central Asia led to confrontation with Abbasid Caliphate
Frankish consolidation led to Carolingian dynasty establishment
